md: An exponent is just shorthand for repeated multiplication. $a^3$ is a tidy way to write $a \times a \times a$, three copies of $a$ multiplied together.
Once you picture an exponent as **a count of factors**, the product rule stops being a rule to memorize and becomes something you can just *see*.

blocks.title: The claim
blocks.body: Multiplying powers of the same base adds the exponents:  $\;a^m \cdot a^n = a^{m+n}.$

md: Below, each power is drawn as a row of factor blocks. $a^m$ is $m$ blocks; $a^n$ is $n$ blocks. Slide $m$ and $n$ and watch what happens when the two rows are pushed together.
There is no trick here. $a^m$ means $m$ copies of $a$ in a row:
Write $a^m$ next to $a^n$ and you have $m$ copies followed by $n$ more copies, all multiplied together. Count the whole row: it is $m + n$ copies of $a$.

blocks.tex: a^m \cdot a^n = \underbrace{(a \cdots a)}_{m}\,\underbrace{(a \cdots a)}_{n} = \underbrace{a \cdots a}_{m+n} = a^{m+n}

blocks.title: Watch the base
blocks.body: The blocks only merge into one row because every block is the same letter $a$. The rule needs a **matching base**: $a^2 \cdot a^3 = a^5$, but $a^2 \cdot b^3$ will not combine, because the blocks are different things and cannot be counted as one pile. $\blacksquare$
